results.english:
...greater balance. Perhaps this serenity itself is the highest aim of the practice of Kriya.

My seeking and finding, at last, defeated the initial confusion. By stilling the mind, Kriya dissolved doubt. And if the process unfolds correctly, every remaining answer will come from the sadhana itself. I practice simply because I must — there is no other motivation than the duty owed to my own Self. Always present in the thoughts and hearts of his disciples, Guruji watches over us. Rarely intervening, he simply observes and reflects our mental states, like a mirror — so that we can see ourselves clearly, without distortion.

The Philosophy of Healing and the Healing of a Philosopher

Daniela L., Mexico, Canada

The postmodern philosopher [footnote: Postmodernism — a late-twentieth-century movement in philosophy and literary theory. It usually questions the basic propositions of Western philosophy from the 17th to the 19th centuries. Put simply, postmodernism declares: since people's opinions change and one person's opinion cannot be more correct than another's, a specific work of art is no better than any other. This means art, music, literature, and religion can be whatever their creator or follower calls them.] Jacques Derrida [footnote: Jacques Derrida (15.07.1930 – 09.10.2004) — a French philosopher of Algerian origin, known for developing the philosophy of deconstruction. One of the principal authorities associated with poststructuralism and postmodernist philosophy, though he himself never used the word 'postmodern.' One of his best-known works is the book L'Animal que donc je suis ('The Animal That Therefore I Am').] wrote in his book The Animal That Therefore I Am that humans are autobiographical animals, capable of finding their own traces, telling the stories of their lives, erasing or recreating their paths. Looking back over what I have lived, I feel I have worn down many different pairs of shoes, driven by the desire to find the secret meaning of Life. The play of duality, the contrasts of light and shadow, the eternal saga of the feminine and the masculine, the riddles of the sensed and the invisible — all of this fed my investigative and experimental attempts to apprehend reality. I fed my mind impressions like a hungry chick, waiting for it to turn into wisdom. And I suffered from impatience. I needed a signal, a justification for why I should be, why I should exist.

I was a wanderer, experimenting with the instruments available to modern seekers: plant medicines, traditional ceremonies, sacred dances. All these sources plug into a universal current of energy, and in it we are one. That current does not need to know 'Why' in order to be — it is itself the cause of being. And yet, none of these experiences would assemble into a map by which I could trace my own steps. I felt lost, scattered. And I observed many like me — wandering, restless, endlessly ruminating and talking too much. Fixated on their own 'I' rather than seeking to know their own 'Self.'

When I chose to study philosophy, I thought it was the right tool, capable of helping me overcome the constant sensation of the abyss, the all-consuming feeling of Non-being that had stalked me since childhood. So philosophy became my life for a long time. Diving into it, I breathed it in and savored how it calmed the mind with a vision of purpose. But in the end, what allowed me to open up to the world also made me harden, made me 'set.' And again I felt cut off from others, and on top of that obliged to 'extract value' from my study of philosophy. None of this is new — many pass through similar experiences in search of salvation, needing help, fighting inner demons, longing for healing.

It seems my 'hardening' reached its peak one especially grim, snowy morning in Quebec. That day I walked into a yoga studio across the street from the university where I was studying. It was my thirty-third birthday, and, gathering my courage, I finally decided to step into the unknown and plunge into Ashtanga yoga in the Mysore style [footnote: Mysore is a style of Ashtanga-vinyasa yoga in which each practitioner performs a set sequence of asanas at his or her own pace while the instructor only corrects and assists when needed.]. Yoga was meant to be the medicine for my inner restlessness. I hoped it would be effective, unlike theory or another set of metaphysical formulas. This yoga turned out to be an almost martial physical discipline, an ideal method for balancing my hidden nature, the wild and crazy part of me. From one extreme I threw myself into another. From intellectual attempts to grasp existential laws and their interrelations — to the rigorous discipline of a body that knew far more ancient secrets than the limited intellectual, logical mind. The body, however, was in no hurry to surrender its secrets.

In that studio, the Mysore Shala [footnote: Shala — in Sanskrit shala means house, dwelling, abode. In the context of yoga, it is a school of yoga, a yoga space, a yoga institute.], I met a team of remarkable teachers, and the new experience changed everything for me. Three months later I said goodbye to my academic career, deciding to become a yoga teacher. Nothing of the sort had ever been in my plans, and before this I would have considered such a turn absolute madness… right up to the moment when it became my new reality. I began assisting in classes, and yoga began binding together many things from my past: my interest in dance and movement, even philosophy…

One day I overheard teachers talking about Kriya yoga, mentioning their Guru, India, sadhana — but not discussing the practice itself, only the changes that came with it. I listened carefully. Ashtanga yoga was containing my 'madness,' but I wanted to uncork the lid so it could come out for good.

The theme of healing ran like a red thread through the fabric of every story unfolding around me; consciously or unconsciously I was still searching for an effective medicine. The time had come to start taking it. The moment was ideal — the shadow of mid-life crisis was already beginning to follow me. There was nothing to lose. All that remained was absolute faith in the discipline of practice as an antidote to the abyss of nihilism. Though my ideas about the particulars of this yoga were rather vague.

Deciding to spend wisely the last savings remaining in my bank account after my inglorious departure from academia, I bought a ticket to India. Crazy, isn't it? That country had never interested me, and I could never have imagined ending up there. As I boarded the plane, the leaves in Quebec were just beginning to turn yellow.

Twenty hours later I walked out of the Delhi airport building. A full moon was rising, the Indian night threw a veil of mist over my shoulders, and I felt almost comfortable. Alone, immediately lost, with no working phone and no numbers to call, I climbed into a taxi with a stranger speaking a foreign language to go I knew not where. It was that very shift of phase, when the impossible becomes real. The only wish was for a new version of myself, one that would respond to life differently. Sitting in the back seat, embracing my fears, I watched the sun slowly dissolve the mist, opening up a strange new world. The drive was long, the sounds of unfamiliar speech mixed with the cacophony of the approaching day and the storyboard of slides of local daily life flashing past the window.

…A week later I was recalling those moments, doing my first Kriya practice in a slightly dusty palace room that smelled of incense and bats. Everything here was beyond what is considered ordinary; everything in this ancient portal to death spoke of life and breathed it: stones and trees, insects and bands of unruly monkeys. Every bird, plant and animal coexisted here in a strange, naked, vivid harmony.

On the day of my arrival I asked Guruji for initiation, and he warned me of the difficulties of the path. That suited me — I had to follow a goal I barely understood. I wanted the new discipline to direct my daily actions, and I saw in it a path toward a new perception of inner freedom. Though the Guru said that the very concept of 'freedom' is incomprehensible to many, and I tried to take that in. At that time it was expressed beautifully by the words of Janis Joplin: 'Freedom is just another word for nothing left to lose.'

It so happened that this practice became for me the most engrossing experiment in faith in the unknown. An experiment for which I bear full responsibility, one I still cannot fully understand. It seems this yoga is the goal of all my actions taken together. Nobody imposed this practice on me, and I do not worry much if for some reason I miss a day or two. Kriya is akin to building a fire: you need enough wood, you must watch the direction of the wind or rain. All these concerns lie within the sphere of my responsibility. Sadhana kindles the inner flame, burning up the rubbish of the everyday, purifying perception, birthing creativity rather than suppressing it. Throughout my travels Kriya remained my constant companion. Even if at times practice was impossible because of journeys or illness, the desire to find a time and place in which I could return to yoga was the burning arrow I followed through the dark.

My visions began again (or rather, the inner Vision switched on again). Yes, I see certain things, and this capacity is part of me. Sometimes it is palpable energies or perfectly distinguishable auras, glowing cocoons of subtle bodies, contours or lightning of impulses, both mental and physical. I had partly experienced this in dance practices.

When I was fifteen, I first experienced an altered state of consciousness at a rave, remembering the precise moment when consciousness flipped from clarity to the state in which the medicine (acid) began to act. The wall behind my back began to 'breathe' with every pore, every molecule of its surface, and I turned to a friend: 'Do you feel this? This whole place is breathing!' She answered, 'Yes!'

Then, for the first time, I realized that our bodies are subtle and fragile shells, and that they are not us. In that period of life the body felt like something heavy, burdensome, and the realization that I was not my body utterly astonished me, becoming in many ways liberating. And that liberation led me to various healing practices, and not always with the help of stimulants. The visions allowed me to see different things, not just to get high and watch colorful pictures, but to receive knowledge at a subconscious level.

Some may argue about the value of such methods, but I feel no need to justify myself. I rarely discuss these experiences at all, preferring to keep them to myself as deeply intimate experiences connecting me with Life. In a textbook of Hindu philosophy I read about many paths leading to the summit of the mountain. Substances as healing tools helped me catapult into inaccessible places, providing the gear with which I could then climb on my own. Fortunately, during my wanderings I met people who gave me clues helping me to navigate. At some point the path led me to the south of Mexico, where the earth was joined by its umbilical cord to the Moon.

…The Mexican abuelas, the elder-women, have long been gathering women's circles of wisdom. There are differing theories about the origin of this tradition, and much of what is known now reaches back through millennia, into archives preserving images of ritual calendars and descriptions of ceremonies. These documents, or Codex, were 'gathered up' by Catholic priests after the Spanish invasion. In our day these rites have been revived under the guidance of the abuelas, who have gained access to the hidden meaning of the Codex and reclaimed this wisdom. results.english:
...the opportunity for self-healing through nine-year initiations. The ritual includes fasting and dancing, once a year, at full moon.

And again I followed an insistent inner voice that at first I refused to listen to, because usually it is the cause of painful changes. Nevertheless, I took part in the ceremony. Imagine two hundred women of all ages, dressed in white, dancing under the full moon for four nights. The dance unfolds in four directions, folding into geometric patterns recalling four serpents intertwining and swallowing each other. Each direction of the circle represents specific attributes and energies of the healing wheel that each woman works with on a personal and collective level. The sacred songs in the ancient Nahuatl language I do not understand, but the body absorbs and remembers them, and the heartbeat of the enormous drum, played by drummers seated in a circle, guides the dancers. Each fragment of the dance possesses its own power, each engages various aspects of the elements and switches on the energy needed for healing and harmonization. The healing force of movement, dance, song, and fasting combined become instruments of transformation. On one of those nights I saw a mandala open in the sky, and from it emerged myriads of Ganeshas [footnote: Ganesha — one of the most revered Hindu deities. Son of Shiva and Parvati, elephant-headed, remover of obstacles, god of fertility, patron of arts and wisdom.] — it was strange and stirring to see them on another continent, summoned by other tribes, other traditions.

The mind poured into a multidimensional space, where the question 'Why this Life?' found its answer in the form of visible energy. Such things had previously arisen only in the practice of Kriya, where visions came from the middle of the radiant Kutastha. The screen of the dark circle opens many portals; it is never empty there, and something is always happening: folding geometric figures, shimmering many-colored lights. Earlier I could enter this space only through the portal of ceremonial healing plants. Now I think Kriya, too, is a kind of medicine.

This yoga drew me because it is what I must do. Not because it is something I 'consume,' or that carries me into a world of dreams. No, Kriya is far deeper. It helps to dispel my darkness, to focus on what I draw out of what I have seen, to connect with this mysterious portal. Honestly, I cannot explain it more clearly, since I am speaking about this for the first time.

Where will this striving toward self-healing lead me? As often as I can, I return to Govardhan, where I practice in the dark halls of the palace and every day see Guruji, drinking in the generosity of his answers and watching the other disciples. The place and the processes it sets in motion gradually allow me to meet myself. The smashan tells me: 'I see you; have the courage to see yourself and to keep going. The healing of what troubles your spirit lies within you.' And I feel boundless gratitude.

Sometimes I am asked why I practice, who Shailendra Sharma is, and what his super-powers are. Although I do not think he levitates at night, I know no one can be completely sure of that. Anything is possible. But seriously, I feel that Guruji's super-power lies in his absolute devotion to the practice, in his life at this ancient smashan, and in the unshakeable might with which he holds and directs energies invisible to others during the nightly puja. In a world where any fashion or trend lives no longer than five minutes, the Kriya discipline that the Guru follows is supremely dependable.

This yoga is not only a healing medicine, but also an inspiration to keep moving, to live. It is precisely that inspiration that made me take full responsibility for the practice that lets me glimpse my immortal substance. That which lives within me, beyond the matter of which I am made, and we all are.

One day I would like to dissolve into infinity. I hope it does not sound too pretentious, but even if it does, I will not apologize for the pathos. Rather, I am simply inviting you to smile at the length of the path I still have to walk.

My goal in Kriya goes beyond the intention to become better. This Goal lies in awakening the subconscious, and it is to transform consciousness. Perhaps I will meet my true 'I,' though to this day I have no idea what that may mean.

Do I think this is an occult practice? Yes. It nourishes the alchemy of spirit, and there is nothing more occult.

Accepting one's own humanity is the most honest thing one can do. I am perfectly imperfect, and accepting this fact has brought me absolute freedom. Perfect in my imperfection, I keep going. And yes, we are 'autobiographical animals,' we reinvent ourselves and cover the old tracks, but it is impossible to hide them entirely. Everything remains. Within us lie dormant powerful memories, the DNA of ancestors, everything that happened in past generations, before us. Events for which we are not responsible are recorded within us and define the imprint we leave. Today we continue to carry ancient spirits in the modern disposable containers of bodies. Without an understanding of the personal past, it is impossible to step into the future — and the Guru teaches us to read the history of our Spirit. Such understanding can change the future.

…This truth rang for me like a bell during a healing ceremony with a sacred plant. Mother Aya said: 'You descend from a race of colonizers; the responsibility rests upon you to accept this and to correct your actions, in order to heal the past. Then you will find peace.' All these revelations led me to Mexico, where I now live, very far from India. But every night in practice I see the flame of the Guru's dhuni and the ghee he pours into the fire. I perceive this as a healing remedy for the world and for myself.

The Pujari

Thin, wiry, with a smoothly shaven head, he wore heavy black kundalas made of the same rhinoceros hide as the Guru's kundalas. The pujari resembled a djinn, though he served no one. In the hot summer months he wore the same well-laundered dhoti and an ancient kurta whose greyish fabric had long since lost its original whiteness. The ensemble was completed by heavy necklaces of large rudrakshas. His daily bicycle trips for puja supplies had an almost hypnotic effect on pilgrims passing by. And although the dry features of his dark face expressed nothing, even the locals tried to avoid meeting the pujari's eyes, unwilling to be reflected in his utterly emotionless gaze, grey like the sky in monsoon season. Those who accidentally did catch his eye felt small and insignificant. Not because he was challenging or expressed superiority, simply because he was filled with knowledge of human imperfections — including his own. In the past, Narendra Sharma had worked with metal and was well acquainted with all the possible properties of iron, steel, or aluminum objects. And he knew that metal is sturdier and more reliable than people.

One day a nephew brought him to a young yoga Guru living near Gwalior. At that time Narendra was suffering from a severe cough caused by his excessive fondness for smoking charras, and nothing brought relief. Having observed the man with the absent gaze that did not even change during fits of violent coughing, the yogi gave him some medicine that healed him in three days. That was the first problem the Guru solved, but not the last. Narendra Sharma began visiting the yogi regularly. He was a man of peculiar disposition, expecting nothing good from life or people in general, but in the Guru's presence his heart was filled with a peace he had not felt in many years.

On the 2nd of October 1992, he tossed the keys to the shop to his brother and got into a passing truck to Vrindavan, where the Guru had temporarily settled in a small ashram. From there they moved for a few months to Sarnath, and in 1994 he came to the smashan. Helper, manager, comrade-in-arms — he worked hard. When the Shivalingam was found and installed, Narendra took on the preparations for the puja and renovated the old wooden pavilion that they had decided to convert into a temple.

Ghee, milk, yogurt, honey, garlands of fresh marigolds, roses or jasmine, datura blossoms and incense were purchased daily and prepared before the ritual twice a day. Afterward, the temple had to be carefully cleaned, and all this was his responsibility. When the Guru kindled the fire in the dhuni, he would blow the bull's horn, then strike the drum and light the oil lamps. There were no holidays, because every day at the smashan was already a respite from worldly life…

On the day Narendra Sharma received the news of his mother's death, he set out for Delhi for the cremation ritual, but returned in time for the night puja, since he could not miss it. Preparing for the cleansing ritual of prayashchitta, he shaved his head, and his resemblance to Mahatma Gandhi was deepened by the round-framed reading glasses he occasionally wore. It was the Guru who, in jest, began calling him 'Mahatma-ji,' and the nickname stuck. Narendra did not object. Gradually, visitors coming to the Guru began to show him respect as well, touching his feet. For the most part he remained indifferent, saving his rare smiles for old friends.

After the death of Devahuti, Mahatmaji fell into a stupor. She had been the cement holding their life on the smashan together, the joyful force behind the daily events, the well-oiled motor ensuring the uninterrupted, smooth running of the mechanism. It was she who looked after Mahatmaji, sometimes teasingly reminding him to change his dhoti, to eat on time, to smile at the gossip. Apart from his mother and sisters, she was perhaps the only woman he respected. He felt no particular regard for other women.

The day after her cremation he sat by the pond, looking at the still surface of the water, and his face was wet with tears. The male antelope known as Gulab Singh approached the pujari and sniffed his shoulder. Gulab had lived on the estate a couple of years; Devahuti had often fed him the sweet flowers of the red hibiscus. Mahatmaji startled and waved his hand to shoo the animal away. Alone in this country of cows and people, Gulab Singh stepped back and slowly turned toward the kitchen, his hooves clicking softly on the stone slabs.

For the period of mourning the pujas were stopped. The Guru recovered slowly from the loss. Weekly visits from his niece with her newborn daughter helped the healing process. Lying on the Guru's lap, the child would gaze up at him gravely and occasionally smile. It was not the meaningless bubbling grimace of an infant, but the smile of an old friend. At least, that is how it looked. In the end the Guru began to smile back.

Nearly two years passed; the little girl had grown and was already running about everywhere when a large statue of Devi made of gleaming brass was installed in the southern hall of the house. A red carpet was laid on the stone floor, the previously open terrace was completely glazed in and became part of the house. There they installed a stone altar from which the Goddess gazed at visitors approaching the house along the main avenue. In the early evening the sweet aroma of incense filled the warm air, mingling with the scent of flowers, and the flame of oil lamps was reflected in hundreds of black-and-white diamonds with which the eyes of Devi were inlaid.

A new, powerful presence had established itself on the smashan,

results.english:
and it was different from the intoxicating energy felt before. Perhaps because the source now resided in the statue of the Goddess; yet everyone was so affected by the waves of this power that no one bothered with the details and causes of the phenomenon…

Meanwhile Mahatmaji withdrew ever more into himself. Not particularly talkative before, he was now silent for entire days at a time. By the summer of 2017 he had grown so thin that his clothes nearly slid off him. Like a silent ghost, limping, he made his way around the temple, preparing for the evening rituals, but mostly stayed in his room.

The sound of the bull's horn that he blew before puja became weak and rasping, as if a sick bird were trying to clear its throat. He stopped shaving his head, and a mane of snow-white hair reached down to his bony shoulders. Only two months younger than the Guru, the pujari looked at least twenty-five years older. Some strange emptiness was devouring him, but no one could identify the cause. Perhaps the term 'existential crisis' would have been apt, though it would have sounded too elaborate. Mahatmaji began skipping meals and lived on tea and chapatis alone. On such days the Guru would ask the workers whether the baba had eaten anything, whether he had left his room that day…

Sometimes the Guru would play him songs from old Bollywood films on his iPod, and for a while the pujari would come back to life and even try to smile weakly. He grew more and more absent-minded; during puja the Guru had to remind him of skipped sequences and forgotten ingredients.

At one point the Guru asked him directly: 'Maybe we should arrange a marriage for you? A woman could take care of you, manage the cooking and laundry…' The pujari shook his head in silent refusal.

In July 2017, during the puja to Hanuman, the large decorative mace that normally rested on the gilded bas-relief of the deity suddenly moved. Rolling by itself from left to right, it returned again to its previous position. This minor event would certainly have gone unnoticed by visitors, but the Guru had lived on the smashan too long and paid close attention to the smallest occurrences. Hanuman, the protector of ascetics and observant brahmacharis, was clearly giving some kind of sign, but the meaning of the message remained unclear for several months more. At the end of September, after the evening puja, Mahatmaji spoke. Without raising his eyes, he told the Guru that he had entered into a liaison with a married woman of the sweeper caste, and she was pregnant. A scandal was inevitable; he wished to leave the smashan immediately.

When the pujari finished, the Guru asked a purely practical question: 'What are you going to do in the world after more than twenty years of life here?' Mahatmaji answered that he still remembered his trade and could earn a living. The Guru told him that, for the pujari, it would be preferable to stay and face the consequences, however heavy.

Mahatmaji said nothing. The next morning he disappeared without a word to anyone. Surfacing in Gwalior, he first stayed in the house of one of the disciples, then wandered for some time from one acquaintance to another. He remained mostly silent; he also stopped cutting his hair and nails. Sitting all day in the little shops of his temporary benefactors, he made an unforgettable impression on visitors with the shock of grey hair, the thick beard, the worn clothes, and the somnambulistic expression. He did not take off the kundalas, explaining that he feared the ghosts of the smashan, who might reach him even here. Once he was seen at the railway station, gazing through the scurrying crowds with the same absent expression. Someone remembered that his brother had committed suicide by throwing himself under a train. But nothing more tragic than life itself befell the former pujari. Whether he had lost his mind or was lost inside it, or both together, Narendra Sharma went on living. After a time Guruji arranged a rented room for him and gave him a small sum of money for expenses.

The place where Mahatmaji had sat for many years opposite the Guru in the darshan hall now stood empty. The wall on which the pujari used to lean still preserved a faint outline of his shoulders and an almost invisible imprint of his nape. But the silhouette was painted over during the next renovation — the walls and columns of the room again became spotlessly white. Now the pujari's usual place was taken by random visitors, and during puja the Guru was assisted by a new helper. When asked about the pujari, the Guru gave the same terse answer: 'He has retired.' The door to Mahatmaji's room in the palace was locked, and after a while it became as nameless as the others.

Human dramas are often as ordinary as everyday feats. Merits accumulated over years of austerity and obedience can burn up in one slip, like dry hay. Motives of action grow from the seed of past karmas, and the abundance of the harvest depends on the thoroughness of care and attention.

Now before puja the Guru himself blew into the gold-inlaid bull's horn, and the thin blade of sound pierced the darkness.

With disciples, 2010

Discipline

* * *

'Practice is the foundation and pillar of my life. The practice of Yoga has made me who I am. It is sadhana that continues to reveal the essence of things and events — elusive but at the same time obvious and comprehensible to the inner Knower.'
Shailendra Sharma, From Conversations with Disciples

The Guide. The Man and the Myth

The smallness of a man is regarded as the inevitable condition of his greatness. What a man achieves in a day may be infinitely small, and yet it is crucial that he does this small thing. Daily, weekly, monthly, yearly, he kept conducting pujas to Shiva, practicing, teaching, and the schedule remained unchanged regardless of circumstance. In essence, circumstances were forced to adapt to his schedule.

It is difficult enough to maintain daily what has already been created and achieved. The adrenaline of dreaming, desiring, and anticipating helps one reach the goal, but to keep living in a state of eternal striving for triumph is hard. The mind, having reached its goal, grows weary of the monotonous diet. Yet sadhana helped him avoid the traps of ego, satiety, and boredom; daily service and yoga were the guarantors of progress. He remained an industrious student of Life. Even when it seemed that questioning had been exhausted, information continued to come in, and his work was to turn it into Knowledge.

The strongest feeling in every being is the sense of self, 'I am.' It is said it arises with the first breath, but the Self exists before the individual soul takes physical form. Regardless of all achievements, regardless of how low a person falls or how high he strives to rise, the sense of 'I' is unchanged. Bodily sensations and mental conclusions send signals to the brain, and the brain sends the body confirmation of its existence. This is the first and most ancient link the 'driver' establishes with the 'vehicle.'

Any physical shell inevitably passes into non-being, but the soul again and again pulls the handle of the door into life. The Self travels through all these stage sets and uses a diverse range of props. It has already seen so much that it is not surprised by losses, wars, defeats, hunger or plenty. Almost everything has been met, felt, and experienced by the spirit in its previous incarnations. And yet it strives to know what is new, untangling the karmic knots of the past and fulfilling its assigned mission.

…All these years he had studied and known intimately the aspects of his own personality, its roots, karmic connections, hidden traumas, and 'blind spots.' Gathered together, this understanding crystallized into a complete acceptance of self. And yet he retained the distinct feeling that the present 'I' was not the radiant Spirit he had met in sadhana. Closer contact had to be established with that Spirit, in the most literal sense. And as soon as that occurred, the identity of the spirit was established, and the answers to many questions arrived of themselves. The subconscious, awakened by persistent sadhana, became both Teacher and Knower. It was the subconscious that allowed him to explore some of his previous lives, and he did so with interest and a certain trembling. Thus came the confirmation that throughout many incarnations he had been a yogi. This was no discovery; he had already known it since he was five years old.

Whenever he happened to sit by a fire, he was certain that in the past he had tended a flame for some ritual. Of course, the ancestors of all people once sat by fires — in caves, at hunting halts, in nomad camps — but memory brought him echoes of mantras and the images of deities. Most often he saw himself by a dhuni; his form changed from life to life. The hair was always long, since he could feel the touch of strands of jata against his back. Sometimes the body was smeared with clay, sometimes with ash; the kamandalu, the ritual water vessel that stood beside him, was made of coconut, of wood, or sometimes of stone. And yet the most recent incarnation was hidden from him. However hard he looked at the surface of the past, hints were absent. Information about his last birth, approximately between 1893 and 1957, seemed deliberately blocked. Most likely the Teachers (Babaji himself or the masters of the parampara) had decided not to reveal this information so that he could carry out his present mission without being distracted by echoes of the recent past. Moreover, memories of the recent life might have restrained the course of events in the present. What mattered most was that that last life had led to the present one; that was enough.

Of course, acquired experience, upbringing, education, and worldview exert a powerful influence on personality. Only all this becomes secondary from the moment the stream of Absolute Mind passes through the body of a yogi who has attained samadhi, forever altering the settings of perception. His personal path was predetermined because somewhere in the past he had chosen the role (or had been chosen for it) in the present play.

There is no smooth and frictionless spiritual path. Individual karmic preconditions set in motion the mechanisms of decision-making, create new chains of reactions, and only spiritual education helps to discern the results. Karmic reprogramming requires aspiration, will, and the help of Divine Providence. Everything is just as the Bhagavad Gita describes: 'The five causes that bring every action to completion are the body, the doer, the various senses, the various endeavors, and the fifth — Divine Providence. Whatever action a person performs with heart, word, or body, righteous or not, these five are the cause of his actions.'

…One of his favorite books, with the simple but resonant title The Guide, was written by R.K. Narayan. It described the story of a swindler-tour-guide, 'Railway Raju,' who against his own will became a respected sadhu. The novel had everything: crime, punishment, recognition, and exposure. The book had been published just a year after his own birth,

results.english:
...and when he was nine he saw the film made from the novel and was utterly charmed by it. Having read The Guide in adulthood, he remembered many passages; the subtle irony of a swindler's transformation into a spiritual leader did not escape him. The Guide… Who is guiding whom — and, more importantly, where? In India, even spiritual swindling could lead to revelation. In a certain sense, he found a likeness to the character in his own story: people peered at him just as they peered at Raju, who was as a result declared by them to be a saint. Some came timidly and reverently, others insistently and with frank curiosity — all longed to find in the Guru both the divine and the human. They needed something with which to align their own social and moral coordinates, something that could show them the way and give them hope. Recognizing the human sides of the Guru brought relief, while a manifestation of the divine was received with awe and even fear. Could he see through them, read thoughts and the future? He could, but he did not, respecting people's privacy.

He recognized human archetypes, the repetition of behavioural algorithms, and at the same time was struck by the unpredictability of human action. Human nature itself is quite predictable, but the reactions of different people to the same circumstances differed dramatically.

What then would remain if a hypothetical Guru were stripped of all deification? He thought that, by focusing excessively on the person of the spiritual Teacher, people might miss the important meeting with the principal Guru, Life itself. The seeker needs to look no less attentively into Her eyes, to listen to Her instructions. The human aspect of the teacher's personality is always permeated by personal experience, coloured by the specifics of culture, time, and place. And only Life Herself knows absolutely all the answers; embodied teachers at best can help one make one's way through Her labyrinths and emerge onto a plateau of new possibilities.

The Indian guru-disciple system is far more autocratic than modern European society realises. The seeker is the child, and the teacher is the father, regardless of the ages of both. Even when the child grows up and goes out into the world, the role of the father remains in force. Reception of the teaching requires self-discipline, and there is always the faint shadow of the fear of disappointing the spiritual parent. Like any child, the seeker learns by himself to discern goals and reach them. Such a way of upbringing is difficult, and encouragement is just as necessary as severity. Devotion to the Teacher is cultivated not in order to strengthen attachment or prove personal loyalty, but as a step on the path to liberation. Some disciples advance no farther than a certain level and do not seek emotional independence; they feel safe under the protection of the Teacher, whose personality and guidance may become a kind of magnet, anchor, or drug.

As in earlier times, the personality of the Guru influences the creation of a clear coordinate-system for those who are trying to orient themselves spiritually in this complicated modern world. But how greatly does the Guru-disciple system differ from religious dogma? Especially in a culture where the Guru is sometimes perceived as a living Deity? The beauty of the relationship between a spiritual Teacher and his disciples lies in the fact that the Guru, too, is a child, learning through his disciples. Yes, the Teacher helps to solve the riddles of existence, and sometimes himself needs help and protection, an indirect lesson or a veiled hint. After all, he too is human. If there is no friendly relationship between the disciple and the spiritual teacher, if there is no acceptance of the Guru's fatherly role, the most important element in the transmission of knowledge is absent, and the journey may turn 'into the wrong place.'

So long as illusions and obscurations have not vanished entirely, the yogi must carefully follow the Guru's instructions. The modern variety and availability of information leads people to believe that a careful compilation of facts can provide the necessary maps for the spiritual journey, and some decide to advance without a guide. In the light of the Guru-Shishya concept (the tradition of the transmission of knowledge from teacher to disciple) a self-led path is a myth, since the disciple's achievements and efforts cannot be verified by any existing criteria. Therefore those achievements either remain a product of the imagination or become the rarest of cases, more like a fairy tale.

There is no such thing as independent sadhana. Someone taught the practitioner the mantra or the technique, someone guided him or her — and that someone was the Guru. Regardless of a person's level of literacy, knowledge of the alphabet remains foundational; year after year the practitioner continues to lean on the teaching first given to him by the master. The sadhaka must not compose his own mantras or change techniques, nor try to find a shortcut to enlightenment. More often than not, in cases of independent sadhana the practitioner reaches a dead end, and only the Guru can help him through that stage. In the Indian tradition even the most advanced practitioner does not 'leave' the Guru. Even after the yogi has become wholly self-confident and self-sufficient, the Guru still directs him.

…He could confirm this personally. After the passing of Sri Satyacharan Lahiri he communicated with his teacher only a few times, but he always received the necessary support. The Guru first 'visited' him in the subtle body in 1993, when life at the smashan had only just begun and the threats and attacks could at any moment have grown into a full-scale war. Another time the Guru appeared on the eve of the installation of the Shivalingam. And a few days before the passing of Devahuti, when he was in suffocating depression and the inevitability of the impending loss was painfully obvious. Then, right after one of the night samadhis, Sri Satyacharan Lahiri made his presence known, and the Teacher's words brought comfort. Like the mental messages from Babaji, and the appearance of the Mahavatar on the physical plane — the presence of the Teachers was a priceless milestone that changed his life.

Sadhana

Learning that he had not left the smashan since 1993, some visitors would ask, puzzled, whether he would not like to see the world, to visit different countries. He would answer with a smile: 'I have already seen everything and been everywhere.' To the most insistent he explained that a yogi who has begun the daily ritual and lit the fire of the dhuni must observe the established discipline. The host must be at home if he has invited guests. The spirits invoked nightly by the sounds of the horn would be offended by any interruption of the appointed order and by his absence.

Several times he had gone out to see a doctor or to visit a sick aunt, but the night puja proceeded on schedule. And besides, what would he do out there, in the world? Give lectures, receive flower garlands, and hand out autographs? He could do that here, on the Land of Liberation.

There was no need to spread the good news of Kriya yoga, since there was no shortage of popularizers. People who wished to find the essence of yoga and were not satisfied with the undemanding techniques taught at paid mass initiations could find their way to him. And they did come. Life itself constantly renewed the gallery of human archetypes, and that at least was interesting. Nearly every day someone new turned up, and, as in fairy tales, only a pure heart was needed to enter the enchanted smashan.

After the puja and the evening tea he would say goodbye to those present: 'Time to get to work.' Practice invariably began at exactly midnight. A schedule once established was easy to maintain. Samadhi was part of life, and life itself had become a continuous Samadhi of varying intensity. The day's duties and the intense night yoga complemented one another, but sadhana was the priority. Everything else was built around it, not the other way round.

The sounds of the day fell silent, darkness thickened, fed by the same force that fed the day. Wrapped in the scent of night-flowers in summer, swaddled in the cold blanket of mists in winter, the Earth slept, listening with closed eyelids to the lullaby of dogs' barking and the tender sobs of owls, and he entered Her dreams. When the heart stopped its rhythm, the body still felt the breath of the great Mother.

The Spirit relished daily life through body, senses, and emotions, anticipating the night 'expeditions,' during which the mind travelled through immense worlds extending far beyond gravitational fields.

The human body always personifies the past. The very fact of birth confirms past karmic ties between the child and the parents. The first cry of the infant signals the start of a short marathon into nowhere. Emerging from the womb, all begin a journey back, to a formless state. Hatching from the cocoon of the past, the present becomes the larva of the future. At first blind and formless, hiding the rudiments of the past, full of potential possibilities. Perhaps because of intrauterine memories (or in spite of them), the present obstinately dreams of becoming the future.

What does all this have to do with the practice of yoga? Most directly: all revelations are the result of comprehending the transformative stages of life, of understanding the source from which all bodies have come… Sprouting from within him, the shoots of Knowledge rose in the field of yogic sadhana, and he gathered the harvest with resolve and gratitude to the Teachers and to Life.

People kept rediscovering fragments of reality instead of witnessing its wholeness. Samadhi was complete cognition, an absolute grasp of the flow of time. To be wholly established in the present meant to observe the very moment of the transformation of past into future. Yoga cut through the waves of the awakened subconscious, breaking them against the shores of probabilities and re-sculpting the terrain.

The eighth limb of yoga, samadhi, was the peak of the difficult evolution of body and mind. It granted total awareness of everything, complete and non-dual cognition of the world. He insisted that it began only after the attainment of the yogic state of suspended animation, breathlessness, the consciousness stepping beyond the body.

Constant presence in the Sound was also an essential part of the connection with the Source. The Sound nesting inside him now led the mind through galaxies, between atoms and particles. It was in this way that, many years ago, the realization of the Void began for him. Catalyzed by the fire and alchemy of sadhana, the mind became extraordinary. Embraced by Time, the Great Void carried all Matter. The unification of phenomena was the beginning of all metamorphoses; all is material and real — regardless of whether it had astral or gross nature.

…In the house it was quiet and dark, but the first birds were beginning timidly to drive out the night. Around 4:30 in the morning the servant prepared the tea and gently knocked on the door, knowing that Guruji was there in the room, but needed some time to return from where he had been.

The Five Promises

By tradition, during initiation the aspirants must give five promises. The list was short, and the vows sounded deceptively simple. First, those who chose to devote themselves to this yoga must practice daily. In case of illness or while travelling, they undertook to practice mentally. The initiated also gave a promise to keep the techniques secret and to read a chapter of the Bhagavad Gita every day. This yoga was for life, and as sometimes happens in life, even the simplest of duties may in time grow burdensome.

All religions presuppose strict adherence to morally established codes, and society ensures their observance. The list of the ten commandments is virtually universal. And yet at times he wondered: if the sin has not yet been committed, is there any need to repent in advance? That part of doctrine remained unclear to him. It seemed that sins are necessary to organized religions as the perfect tool for manipulation. The very concept of original sin rather vaguely conditioned a potential 'salvation,' casting the shadow of guilt even before the action had materialized. So too the promises of heaven or hell seemed too primitive and trivial for the ever-accelerating modernity.

results.english:
For practising yogis the vows established by the parampara are quite enough. The remaining moral compasses were honed and reinforced by sadhana itself. Usually called the Ten Commandments of yoga, the principles of Yama and Niyama [footnote: Yamas and Niyamas — the niyamas and their counterpart, yama, are in yoga the recommended kinds of activity and habits for healthy life, spiritual enlightenment, and the attainment of liberation. They have several meanings depending on context. In yoga they are described in the first two of the eight limbs. The first limb, yama, includes virtuous self-restraint ('non-recommended action'). The second part, niyama, includes virtuous habits, conduct and observance of rules ('recommended action'). These virtues and ethical preconditions are considered necessary for the attainment of the self-realized, liberated state of being (moksha).] were not categorical in their designation of right and wrong, but the virtues they explained led to yoga. Kriya offered the main prize: liberation from rebirth. And it could halt pain, dispel anguish, bring tranquillity. And then a new variety of happiness could fill the heart with quiet bliss.

Unlike other Gurus, he never spoke about Yama and Niyama in a preaching tone. There was no need. Yoga itself was changing the yogi's personality, sadhana became the mirror in which one could study one's own Self, and that reflection gradually became more distinct.

Ahimsa — non-violence, Shaucha — purity of body, mind, intentions, and speech, Satya — truthfulness, Asteya — non-stealing… Aparigraha — absence of greed, is impossible without the inner acceptance of greed as an alien and disturbing emotion.

All these moral principles required no commentary.

Continence, or Brahmacharya, is often perceived as moderation of the senses, but the nature of this state rests on the proper use of energy and refers to the chastity of mind. Control of sexual impulses cannot be forcible — only the mind can transform sexual energies into something more powerful. Continence is the result of yogic sadhana, not of fear of breaking a taboo. In essence, prohibitions are created for the education of immature souls: by transgressing the prohibition, the soul multiplied karmas. Observing temptations, the yogic mind sees their roots and recognizes the dubious results of short-term satisfaction. Discernment of natural impulses cleans the path of rubbish, helping one see that all desires are ignited by the playing Spirit knowing Itself. Desires (whether an inspired longing or a physical attraction) manifest in order to be satisfied, pacified, or dissolved through understanding of their source, through investigation of their nature. They ferment in the flask of the body, and the alchemy of yoga reveals their essence.

One of the vows perplexed Western students: the initiate promised to think of any married woman as a mother or sister. The same applied to women — they had to relate to every married man as a father or brother. The explanation was quite straightforward: 'There must be no illegitimate children in a family; the disgrace of a lineage directly affects society.' Why is this promise as important as the commandments 'do not kill, do not steal'? And what is the danger of illegitimate children appearing? The vow seemed an old-fashioned prejudice, but the yogic tradition insisted on observing the promises given for very weighty reasons. The purity of the lineage's blood ensured its healthy continuation. It was believed that children born out of wedlock are a cause of loss of prosperity, along with other complications. A set of foreign karmas, secretly mixed into the ancestral blood, could cause damage not only to genealogy or the family's reputation.

If a chain of inexplicable events destroyed the family's wellbeing, hidden causes had to be sought. Most often the cause turned out to be illegitimate offspring: the spirits of the ancestors do not accept the sacrificial rituals performed for them by an alien descendant. Even if such a ritual is carried out sincerely or if the performer of the rite does not know of his origin, the spirits of foreign ancestors derive no benefit from it. Whether superstition or not, in India it is firmly believed that the participation of those not by blood belonging to the lineage destroys it. The intentions and influence of the spirits of a different clan can also affect the structure of society as a whole. Yet even one born outside marriage can attain spiritual heights; the circumstances of birth are not a punishment, they cannot stop personal growth and development; the child simply had no other choice but to be born.

Put more simply, practitioners of Kriya yoga promised to respect social discipline and not to increase chaos and suffering. That is the meaning of the vow, and he considered it logical. And he repeated it both to Western and Indian disciples: as part of society, yogis must respect the rules existing within it.

…And what is the meaning of the daily reading of a chapter of the Gita? What good did it bring? Like the daily practice, the reading of the Gita built discipline, and then quantity became quality. The body, tempered by pranayama, in the end engaged the mind, and the mantric rhythms of the Bhagavad Gita revealed their deepest meaning to the heart, since any true cognition is always inner, not intellectual. The meaning of the text opened simultaneously with the practice, and consciousness expanded while the body came to know the nature of Prana.

In his book The Bhagavad Gita from the Standpoint of Kriya Yoga, Lahiri Mahasaya wrote: 'If you repeat the Gita with love and devotion, then the vibrations of its mantras will penetrate you, and, thanks to your sadhana, all the energy will be directed inward, rather than continuing to dissipate into the external world. This is the greatness of the mantras of the Gita, and this can be considered the essence of all religious texts, allowing the attainment of liberation from births and deaths.'

Knowledge

From time to time some of the newcomers spoke of their desire to know God, and he related to this aspiration sympathetically. The wish to touch the Beyond was expressed in different ways, and the word 'God' was only one of the terms. Every being aspires to merger with the source of its own origin, experiencing a sense of loss and separation from 'It.' The inexpressible subconscious pain of duality gives the impulse for the search of consolation, for the longing to find protection in the cradle in which the given individual life was born. Having walked this path, he knew this feeling well.

The question of the existence of God and of the methods of connecting with Him had come up in conversations many times, and his answer remained unchanged. Most mathematical reasoning is based on the supposition: '…suppose that…,' and one then had to apply the right formula to arrive at a conclusion. For him personally, faith was like mathematical induction [footnote: Mathematical induction — a mathematical method used to prove the truth of certain statements for all natural numbers; a way of proving statements depending on a natural argument.]: it is supposed that God exists, along with spirit and soul. All that is needed at this stage is the correct formula, and the conclusion will inevitably follow.

Though the opinion that Kriya yoga helps one come closer to God is widespread, he formulated the aim differently. The aspiration of yoga is not the cognition of the Creator, but the recognition of one's own essence — it was precisely this that guaranteed an entry ticket to the Colosseum of Creation. He did not want to know God; it was enough for him that God knew about him. For those lacking a sense of humour, this might have sounded a little haughty, but he was not concerned with ideological conformity to the norms of society. He and God were on good terms, and each was busy with his own affairs.

Knowing the Creator is only part of self-realization; the real self-awareness is far deeper than dubious religious revelations. Why? Because of the shortage of time, the fleetingness of life, and the availability of laboratory material, one ought to work with what is always to hand — with oneself. According to the Bible, every person is created in the image and likeness of God. To argue with this proposition is pointless, and he suggested to those thirsting to know God that they investigate themselves as the available sample of the Divine. Love of God, devotion and service to God — all these notions and aspirations belonged to the unclear sphere of religion and had nothing to do with the aim of yoga that he followed and that he taught.

It is useless to try to come up with a new definition of Kriya yoga, since the timeless Darshanas of Yoga, written by Maharishi [footnote: Maharishi — a Sanskrit term (formed from the combination of the words 'maha' — 'great' and 'rishi' — 'sage'). It refers to ancient Indian sages, known as 'seers.' This title is intended for those who attain the highest state of consciousness on the path of evolution. It is believed that maharishis are able to bestow holiness and transmit divine knowledge to those who are worthy of it.] Patanjali, did this beautifully. The 'Sadhana-pada' section says: 'Great efforts applied to know the Lord through selfless tapas and self-knowledge are Kriya yoga.'

Many years ago in his commentary on this shloka he wrote that the practice of Kriya means awakening the dormant, boundless consciousness by means of meditation. Then, having concentrated the awakened consciousness on the Lord, one had to apply enormous efforts to know Him. That is why he insisted on the necessity of self-knowledge before the alleged meeting with the Creator. Before making an appointment, one had to awaken the dormant mind by means of the practice of breath.

The Creator's visiting card, Prana, was issued to every being born with a pair of lungs, an inquiring mind, and a heart yearning for knowledge. And the Lord waited patiently, since He was Time itself and the Void, and this hypostasis of the Creator had room enough for all. Kriya explained the essence of God as Prana.

And then there was Vasudeva [footnote: Vasudeva is both a name and a term. The one who dwells in every atom, every living being. In Hinduism Vasudeva is worshipped as the supreme Deity. The term itself means that all beings dwell in this supreme being and that He Himself is present in all beings. In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna is also called by the name Vasudeva (because he is the son of Vasudeva and Devaki).], the immortal spirit of the entire universe,

[footnote: Vasudeva — see footnote above]
and he taught people to see Him in their own hearts. Foreign disciples often asked who and what Vasudeva was. The answers varied, since they had to correspond to the developmental level of the questioner. Yet the essence of the answers was always the same: he uttered the same words that the Teachers of the parampara had uttered before him. Vasudeva existed everywhere. He was called Param Atma, the highest universal Soul, pervading and supporting all Creation. The fundamental principle of all things and phenomena, Vasudeva was the essence of Life itself in all Her manifestations. To 'see' Him in the heart during practice was the highest attainment. It meant to recognise Prana as the source of life — since Vasudeva is the very essence of Life.

When he saw Him for the first time, the stopped heart allowed the bud of an immeasurable rapture to unfold. From then on Vasudeva dwelt in the garden of his heart every night, and that 'presence' had certain prerequisites and conditions.

…The tradition of Babaji and ancient texts such as the Yoga Darshanas describe two specific stages of samadhi, characterized by different physical states. In the initial stage the yogi sinks into suspended animation, which is a 'safety technique' for the organism. In the state of suspended animation the subconscious awakens, but the nervous system can

results.english:
be damaged by the enormous charge of energy. The body has to be 'switched off' to prevent the burnout of the nervous 'network.' The 'switched-off' body falls into hibernation, the pulse and heartbeat disappear, and this state of complete suspended animation is the first stage of samadhi.

As constant practice strengthens the nervous system, methodical repetition develops the capacity to withstand the accumulating energy. While the mind experiences the state of samadhi, the body functions as usual, without falling into suspended animation. At this stage the nervous system can endure the current of energy without switching off the body. Even while in samadhi the person remains aware of what is happening around him and is able to communicate and react. This second stage of samadhi is known as Nirvikalpa-samadhi; another term for it is 'Sa-kriya samadhi,' which means 'to be active in samadhi.'

Sa-kriya is not the exclusive feature of Babaji's lineage; the Natha yogis also speak of it. Several times he had personally met people of whom it was said that they had attained the state of Nirvikalpa [footnote: Nirvikalpa samadhi — the highest state of awareness, in which the human ego and samskaras dissolve, and Consciousness remains. Then the material world becomes like a shadow, and the seeker is liberated from it. In Nirvikalpa Samadhi there is no mind in the form we know it. There is only the state of infinite peace and bliss.] or Sa-kriya samadhi. But they never entered the state of suspended animation, their hearts continued to beat, their pulse continued to be present. Yet only the absence of both these signs is the sole reliable confirmation of real samadhi, and so he had well-founded doubts about the truthfulness of the claims regarding the state of these people.

…The experiences of Sa-kriya samadhi began for him after a series of fantastic events of 1996: the encounter with Shiva, the flood, and the first appearance of Hanuman. The list of incredible happenings was so packed that to determine the exact cause of the onset of Sa-kriya samadhi was difficult. It happened in December 1996. One fine day he realized that the mental state in which he usually was during the night practice now continued through the day as well. He was in it during conversations and walks and, as always, reacted instantly to questions during darshan, until he suddenly understood that the answers were coming from a sphere of unconscious, larger Knowledge. At first the Sa-kriya samadhis came in waves; after a while the state became stable and unbroken. There was also an additional factor: now the sound 'Om' was sounding evenly and continuously, drawing him into such elevated states that visitors sitting beside him felt drugged, as though lulled by invisible waves. Regular physical exercises helped him maintain balance; the body needed a daily reminder that it was being counted on, that it still belonged to the material world. Like any equipment, it required regular maintenance.

…Even after the state of Sa-kriya samadhi had stabilized, his sadhana was not interrupted. Regular practice was part of the vows he had taken, and no signals about its cessation were forthcoming. Earlier, while in samadhi, he would usually 'switch off' the body for an hour or two. After Sa-kriya samadhi became stable, the time interval of 'total immersion' shortened to ten or fifteen minutes. Now he could open the door to the Void at any moment, using only Shvasahita pranayama, and that was enough to slake his thirst.

What and Why

Being a Guru for more than 39 years, he still felt himself a beginner; an experienced and in some sense wise beginner, who had glimpsed the horizon in the boundless ocean of yoga, who understood the navigational charts, but who was still learning. Distant lands existed, since other ships had set sail and reached 'those shores' long before his own journey began. In the past other ascetics, undertaking the same austerities, had attained incredible results, demonstrating the triumph of the eternal Spirit over death. Centuries later the guiding star of Kutastha still shone, and he followed it.

First and foremost he was a yogi, and only then a Guru, since he had taken the vows prescribing him to practice before he undertook to teach others. This yoga could be transmitted to seekers only as part of the Guru's power, and that power could come only from the Guru's personal sadhana. The austerity that lasted a lifetime charged his body with currents of almost supernatural energy, and he knew how to transmit part of it to those who had been initiated. Remembering his own difficulties and uncertainty at the beginning of the spiritual search, he taught with understanding and patience. There was no conflict between the duties of the Guru and his personal sadhana; the rest of his affairs revolved around the night practice. Sometimes he would say: had it not been for the obligations imposed on him by the parampara, he would have chosen solitude and obscurity.

…The Spirit unhesitatingly cast off the worn shell of the body, which required constant care and had an expiry date. People died, while their immortal essence went on living. To yogis this dubious bargain seemed unjust: life is too short, it is not enough for cognition of all the depths of yoga, and only a few managed in one lifetime to achieve liberation from subsequent births. And it was no banal stroke of luck (although that, undoubtedly, played its part), but a combination of merits accumulated in past lives, karmic preconditions for realization, and the magic of yoga itself.

Year after year he was asked the same questions: what is Kriya yoga and what is its goal? What is the criterion of 'correct Kriya yoga,' is it measured by the number of samadhis, by the duration of breath retention? Was Kriya an anaesthesia, numbing the torments of existence, was this yoga an antidote to the poison of the everyday? And if so, was it suitable for those who were already relatively happy, at peace with themselves, and simply wished to maintain balance?

Sometimes going into detail, he patiently explained: Kriya is an advanced form of yoga, revealing the practitioner's potential and helping to recognize and realize one's own immortal substance, the Spirit dwelling in the body. This yoga develops consciousness to the level at which the 'possessor' of consciousness grasps the ancient contract between mind and body. If this explanation sounded too complicated, he added: 'What you are practicing is the art that takes you beyond karma. Beyond the conception of happiness and sorrow, into a state of peace.' This was perhaps the rarest of states, since in it was attained the balance of the three principal qualities of nature, the Gunas.

It is believed that Kriya is done in order to know God, but he held that the main aim is a meeting with one's own 'I.' And the Spirit awaited that meeting, guarding the dormant capacities of the subconscious, anticipating its awakening and transformation into a conscious mind. Kriya yoga was intended for explorers attempting to interpret what they already knew intuitively. It was the key to understanding the workings of Creation, the path to knowing the Creator Himself.

Depending on the personalities and motives of the teachers, the versions of the yoga varied. For decades the debates about the 'real,' 'correct,' 'original' Kriya yoga had gone on, techniques being compared and assessed. Comparative analysis did not interest him personally. From his point of view, repeated controlled samadhi was the only criterion indicating the truth of the teaching — and he taught beginners the same yoga that Sri Satyacharan Lahiri and Babaji had transmitted to him. It was precisely this method of self-realization that allowed astonishing results to be achieved, and he needed no other 'proofs' of efficacy. Before leaving the body, Sri Satyacharan Lahiri transmitted to him the six levels and techniques of Kriya in the form in which all the successors of this tradition had practiced them.

When in 1861 Lahiri Mahasaya was charged with popularizing Kriya yoga, the intentions of Babaji left no doubt: this yoga must be returned to laypeople. Whatever techniques Lahiri received then, they were intended for the first generations of practitioners.

127 years later, the Mahavatar instructed him himself, the Fifth Guru, in the advanced kriyas, individually choosing the difficulty level of practice. The recipient had already experienced samadhi, his body was prepared for the serious test of the next levels. What is interesting is that Babaji did not say whether these higher kriyas should be taught to others. The Mahavatar did not give him instructions on dividing the practice into sequential levels and the proportions of inhalations and breath retentions. The advanced kriyas were explained as a single, integral system, and figuring out the stages came down to him. As a Guru he was obliged to teach, and so he divided the techniques into 11 levels for better assimilation. This systematization is based on personal experience and understanding of the limitations and possibilities of the body, and therefore it came naturally. It was then that he established the corresponding proportions for the pranayamas, which only seemed simple but required gradual mastery.

To help students understand the essence of the Kriya method, he compared it to an abundant Indian dinner, the thali, where many dishes are served on one tray. The food is served in abundance for the enjoyment and satisfaction of any appetite. But not everyone can manage the whole portion at once. Yet it was presumed that all eleven levels must be passed in one lifetime, and only the mind could compel the body to do this. It is all a matter of will and intention: one can attempt to swim across the ocean of Samsara or keep drifting in it. He held that even the shortest journey is more useful than sitting in one place.

The first, introductory level of Kriya included seven preparatory techniques. Two exercises for stretching the tongue helped one perform Khechari mudra, then mental pranayama was carried out, helping to improve concentration on the spine at the points of the potential chakras. This was followed by Shvasahita pranayama and Nabhi kriya. After this the practitioner performed Yoni mudra, during which the yogi could for the first time meet his Spirit. And finally the practice was finished with Mahamudras, preparing the nervous system for the higher kriyas. Each exercise had clearly established proportions.

This practice could be transmitted only by a Guru. Why is this so important? The teacher transmits to the disciple both the energy of knowledge and the impulse of intention, the so-called 'sankalpa,' for continuing the practice. During initiation the Guru touches the disciple's spine at the points of the formation of the potential chakras, uttering 'Om' — thus 'connection' is made to the invisible network of Masters. Of course, this part of the ritual can be received skeptically, but the achievements of those who have undergone the traditional initiation speak for themselves.

Regardless of the number of developmental seminars or time spent visualizing the chakras, most people's centres remained inert, unactivated. The ancient techniques of switching on the chakras differed greatly from modern methods. These centres were traffic-lights at the crossroads of energetic super-highways. To activate the embryos of potential and possibility, invisible and dormant in the cerebrospinal fluid, took hard work.

…The Queen of Kriya yoga was considered to be Khechari mudra — the key condition of practice and the blessing for success. Some managed to perform it at once, some mastered the mudra over several months, with others it took years. This technique was the magic pass that opened the way to the higher kriyas. After performing Khechari, rearrangements followed in the techniques, and the student received a new pranayama. Specific mudras were added, and all this was called the 'first level with Khechari.'

A person ready for the second level of practice received it in three stages. First an introductory part, then a specific mantra, and finally the technique of certain movements of the head with an additional pranayama. The second level was aimed at
